commonIssues: Fire blight is the primary disease threat; avoid excess nitrogen, which increases susceptibility. Prune out diseased limbs at least 15 cm (6 in) below visible infection and disinfect pruners between cuts.
Other common problems: pear psylla, codling moth, wood-boring beetles, anthracnose, canker, scab, and powdery mildew.
Vole and rabbit bark damage on young trees; protect trunks.
Pears flower earlier than apples and are vulnerable to late-frost damage to blossoms; site in a sheltered spot protected from late frost.
Most varieties need a second compatible pear variety flowering at the same time for reliable fruit set; some cultivars are only partially self-fruitful.

This is a large tree (10-15 m / 25-50 ft in the ground). Container growing requires a dwarfing rootstock (e.g. Quince C / Quince Eline) and a large pot, roughly 45-50 cm wide and deep (about 90-125 L); the recommendedMinLiters value reflects that container minimum, not in-ground needs.

source.citation: University of Minnesota Extension — Growing pears in the home garden. Full sun; soil pH 6 to 7, well-drained; water newly planted trees weekly the first year, deep water established trees during dry seasons; fertilizer generally unnecessary, apply only if annual new growth is below 18 in, and excess nitrogen increases fire blight; two varieties generally needed for pollination; hardy varieties suit USDA zones 3-4; bare-root planting in early spring, potted after frost danger (May-June); harvest mid-August through late September; fire blight, Japanese beetles, vole/rabbit damage.

University of Minnesota Extension — Growing pears: feeding generally unnecessary; apply compost/fertilizer only if annual new growth falls below 18 in; excess nitrogen increases fire blight susceptibility.

University of Minnesota Extension — Growing pears: bare-root trees planted in early spring when soil is workable; potted trees after frost danger passes (May-June); harvest mid-August through late September; two varieties needed for pollination; hardy varieties suit USDA zones 3-4.

NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — Pyrus communis: 'Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)'; tolerates occasionally dry soil and prefers good drainage; USDA hardiness zones 4a-8b; deciduous tree 40-50 ft tall, 25-35 ft wide, erect spreading form; flowers in spring, fruits summer/fall; pests pear psylla, codling moth, wood-boring beetles; diseases fire blight, anthracnose, canker, scab, powdery mildew.

NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — Pyrus communis: USDA hardiness zones 4a-8b. Zone 4a corresponds to roughly -34 to -32 C; coldHardyMinC set conservatively to -29 C to represent the species/zone-5 range rather than the coldest cultivars. Frost-sensitive blossoms per RHS (pears flower early and are at risk from late frost).
NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — Pyrus communis: deciduous tree, erect spreading form, 40-50 ft tall; frequent disease and insect problems; high maintenance.

UGA Extension (CAES) — Home Garden Pears: plant trees 20 ft apart; sunlight most or all day maximizes fruit; fertilize with 1 cup of 10-10-10 per tree per year of tree age (max 12 cups), half before growth begins and half after fruit set; Orient, Baldwin, Kieffer, Spalding are partially self-fruitful, otherwise plant compatible cross-pollinating varieties; fire blight management — remove diseased limbs 6 in below infection, disinfect pruners with rubbing alcohol or 1:9 bleach:water.

UGA Extension (CAES) — Home Garden Pears: fertilizer applied in two splits per year (half before growth, half after fruit set), supporting a ~180-day base feeding interval for in-ground trees; sunlight most or all of the day.

RHS — How to grow pears: pears crop best in warm, sunny, sheltered position protected from late frosts; newly planted trees watered regularly during dry spells for the first couple of years; container trees need steady moisture through the growing season; ground trees need no regular feeding if mulched, container trees fed with an organic high-potassium liquid fertiliser every fortnight through the growing season; container pot size 45-50 cm (18-20 in) wide and deep on dwarfing rootstock (Quince C / Quince Eline) with loam-based peat-free compost (John Innes No.3); most varieties need another pear flowering at the same time; harvest September-November depending on variety.

RHS — How to grow pears: container trees benefit from feeding every fortnight (≈14 days) with high-potassium liquid fertiliser through the growing season, informing the containerLeachAdjustment; ground trees fed little if mulched.

RHS — How to grow pears: pears flower earlier than apples and are at greater risk of late-frost damage to blossom; site in a sheltered position protected from late frosts. Supports frostSensitive=true and the late-frost commonIssue.

Confidence note: heatStressC (35 C) is an estimate. None of the authoritative sources read (UMN, NC State, UGA, RHS) state an explicit upper heat-stress temperature for Pyrus communis; 35 C is a conservative placeholder consistent with the species' temperate zone-4a-8b range. hotWeatherMultiplier (1.5) and rainSkipThresholdMm (12) are reasonable care-engine defaults extrapolated from 'deep water during dry seasons / very dry spells when fruit is swelling' guidance rather than explicit published figures.
